items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.q: What does freezing do to bacteria in food?
items.correct: Makes them dormant (slows or halts growth), but does not kill them.
items.misconception: Kills all bacteria in the food.
items.why: Students think freezing sterilises food. It just stops bacteria multiplying - they revive when thawed. That's why thawed food should be cooked promptly and not refrozen raw.

items.q: Is HACCP just a cleaning rota for the kitchen?
items.correct: No - HACCP is a system that analyses hazards across the whole process and sets monitored controls at critical points (e.g. cooking temperatures).
items.misconception: Yes - HACCP is simply a schedule saying when to clean each surface.
items.random_wrong: HACCP is a brand of disinfectant.
items.why: Students shrink HACCP down to 'cleaning'. It stands for Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points: identify biological, chemical and physical hazards at every step (delivery, storage, prep, cooking, holding), then set, monitor and record controls where it is critical.

items.q: How many times can cooked leftovers safely be reheated?
items.correct: Once - reheated thoroughly until piping hot (core at least 75°C), then any remainder discarded.
items.misconception: As many times as you like, as long as the food gets hot each time.
items.random_wrong: Leftovers must never be reheated at all.
items.why: Each cool-down and reheat cycle gives bacteria more time in the danger zone (5-63°C), and some toxins are not destroyed by reheating. FSA guidance: cool quickly, refrigerate, reheat ONCE until steaming hot, then bin what's left.

items.q: The 'danger zone' for bacterial growth in °C is roughly:
items.why: It's 5 to 63. “-5 to 5” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which of these is a high-risk food?
items.why: Correct answer: Cooked chicken. “Uncooked dry pasta” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Recommended minimum core cooking temperature in °C?
items.why: Exact answer: 75. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
